title: The Human Empire
text:
The Human Empire stands as the dominant political power in the known world, its authority stretching from the western coast to the mountain passes that mark the eastern frontier. Founded centuries ago when the scattered human kingdoms united against a common threat, the Empire has grown from a military alliance into a sprawling bureaucracy that controls trade, maintains roads, and enforces law throughout human lands.

The Empire operates through a hierarchy of nobles, each sworn to those above them in a chain that leads ultimately to the throne. Taxes flow upward, military obligations flow downward, and justice is administered according to Imperial law. Citizens enjoy certain protections and privileges, while those declared outlaw lose all rights and may be killed with impunity.

Unknown to all but a handful, the current ruler is not the true King. A witch of terrible power has somehow replaced him, wearing his face and ruling in his stead while working toward ends that remain mysterious. The real King is imprisoned somewhere, perhaps in the legendary Immortality Dungeon, waiting for a champion to discover the truth and set things right.

title: The Church of Three Gods
text:
The Church of Three Gods serves as the spiritual heart of human civilization, maintaining temples in every significant settlement and providing guidance from cradle to grave. The faith teaches that Chronos, Gaela, and Morthane are three aspects of a single divine truth: the eternal cycle of time, growth, and transformation that underlies all existence.

Priests of the Church perform the rites that mark life's passages: blessings for newborns, marriages for couples, and funeral services for the departed. They heal the sick, counsel the troubled, and provide charity to those in need. The Church also maintains schools where children learn to read and write, preserving knowledge through generations.

The Church hierarchy wields considerable political influence, advising nobles on matters of morality and mobilizing the faithful when necessary. Critics whisper that the high priests know secrets they do not share with common believers, truths about the gods and the world that would shake the foundations of the faith. Whether these rumors have any basis remains unclear.

title: The Town Guard
text:
The Town Guard represents local law enforcement in settlements throughout the realm. While technically operating under the authority of the Human Empire, each town's guard functions semi-independently under their local captain, adapting Imperial law to local circumstances and priorities. This arrangement sometimes creates tensions between regional authorities and the central government.

Guards patrol the streets, respond to crimes, and maintain order during festivals and emergencies. They know their territories intimately, recognizing faces and tracking the movements of suspicious characters. Most are decent folk trying to keep their communities safe, though corruption and brutality exist, particularly in larger cities where oversight is weaker.

Relations between the guard and citizens depend heavily on reputation. Those known as honest and law-abiding receive respect and assistance. Those with criminal records face suspicion and harassment. The guard maintains bounty boards where citizens can earn coin by dealing with threats beyond the guard's capacity, from monster infestations to organized bandit groups.

title: The Thieves Guild
text:
The Thieves Guild operates from the shadows of major cities throughout the realm, an organized criminal underworld that has elevated lawbreaking to an art form. They control fencing operations for stolen goods, broker information between interested parties, and run protection rackets that extract coin from businesses unwilling or unable to seek official protection.

Despite their criminal nature, the Guild maintains strict codes of conduct. Members do not steal from fellow members or their families. They do not bring unwanted attention through excessive violence or high-profile crimes. They do not cooperate with authorities under any circumstances. Those who violate these rules face punishments ranging from expulsion to execution.

The Guild offers services that the legitimate economy cannot provide. Thieves can acquire items without questions about their origin. Information brokers know secrets that could topple noble houses. And for the right price, certain specialists will solve problems permanently. Those who earn the Guild's trust find doors opening that they never knew existed.

title: The Keepers
text:
The Keepers are a secret organization dedicated to preserving dangerous knowledge and protecting the realm from threats both internal and external. Their existence is unknown to most, their members operating under cover identities while gathering information and preparing for eventualities that ordinary folk cannot imagine.

The Keepers know the truth about the missing King and the witch who has taken his place. They seek a champion capable of exposing the deception and rescuing the true ruler from wherever he has been imprisoned. But they must move carefully, for the witch has her own network of spies and agents, and premature action would only result in the Keepers' destruction.

Joining the Keepers requires proving both capability and discretion. Potential recruits are watched for months before receiving an approach, tested through seemingly innocent encounters that reveal character and judgment. Those who pass these trials learn secrets that change their understanding of the world forever.

title: The Dwarven Kingdoms
text:
The Dwarven Kingdoms endure beneath the mountains, a civilization older than human memory that has perfected the arts of mining, smithing, and engineering. Dwarves are renowned throughout the realm for their craftsmanship, their stubbornness, and their absolute commitment to honoring their word. A dwarf who breaks an oath brings shame upon their entire clan.

Dwarven society is divided between two philosophical factions: the traditionalists who value lineage and hereditary rule, and the progressives who believe leadership should be earned through achievement. This division creates constant tension within the underground holds, though both sides present a united front against external threats.

Dwarves have long memories and longer grudges. Wrongs committed against them are recorded in the Book of Grudges and passed down through generations until satisfaction is obtained. This makes them implacable enemies but also steadfast friends, for favors are remembered as carefully as insults.

title: The Dwarves of Birth
text:
The Dwarves of Birth represent the traditionalist faction of dwarven society, valuing lineage, hereditary rule, and ancient customs above all else. They believe that leadership should pass through bloodlines, that the old ways are the true ways, and that innovation threatens the stability that has preserved their civilization for millennia.

The Birth faction controls the oldest and most prestigious clan-holds, their ruling families tracing unbroken lineages back to the legendary founders. They guard jealously the secrets of ancient crafting techniques, sharing them only with those who prove worthy through years of service and dedication. Outsiders find them difficult to deal with, as their suspicion of anything new extends to unfamiliar faces.

Relations between the Birth faction and humans vary by individual and circumstance. Some traditionalists view humans as useful trading partners, while others see them as upstarts whose brief civilizations are barely worth acknowledging. Earning their respect requires demonstrating understanding and appreciation for dwarven traditions.

title: The Dwarves of Power
text:
The Dwarves of Power represent the progressive faction of dwarven society, valuing achievement, meritocracy, and innovation above tradition and birthright. They believe that leadership should be earned through deeds and skill, that new techniques can improve upon ancient methods, and that isolation breeds stagnation.

The Power faction has embraced trade with outsiders more readily than their traditionalist counterparts, establishing relationships with human merchants and even experimenting with techniques learned from other races. Their forges produce weapons and tools that incorporate novel designs, and their engineers constantly seek ways to improve upon inherited knowledge.

This progressive attitude makes the Power faction more accessible to human visitors, though they are still dwarves with all the cultural expectations that implies. They value competence and results above social niceties, and those who prove themselves through action can advance far in their estimation.

title: The Elven Kingdoms
text:
The Elven Kingdoms have endured in their forest domains for longer than human history records, civilizations of impossible beauty and unfathomable age. Elves are long-lived, wise, and naturally attuned to magic in ways that humans can only approximate through years of study. Their perspective on time differs fundamentally from shorter-lived races.

Elven society is deeply divided on the question of how to respond to human expansion. Some see the younger race as potential partners, worthy of trade and cultural exchange that could benefit both peoples. Others view humans as a plague upon the world, their brief lives and shorter memories making them incapable of the long-term thinking necessary to preserve the natural order.

This division shapes every interaction between elves and the outside world. The diplomats maintain outposts and negotiate treaties, while the isolationists guard the deep forests against intrusion. Travelers who venture into elven lands never know which faction they will encounter.

title: The Elven Isolationists
text:
The Elven Isolationists believe that human expansion threatens everything the elves have built over countless centuries. They watch human cities spread across lands that were forest a few generations ago, see rivers fouled and soil depleted, and conclude that coexistence is impossible. The younger race simply breeds too quickly and thinks too shortly to be trusted with the world's stewardship.

Isolationists patrol the borders of elven territory, turning back trespassers with warnings that escalate to arrows if ignored. They sabotage human logging operations, scatter herds, and generally make life difficult for settlements that push too close to their forests. Some go further, conducting raids that destroy entire villages in regions they consider stolen elven land.

Despite their hostility toward humans as a race, individual isolationists can sometimes be reached. Those who demonstrate genuine respect for nature and elven ways, who prove through action that they understand what is at stake, may earn grudging acceptance. But such trust is rare and easily lost.

title: The Elven Diplomats
text:
The Elven Diplomats believe that cooperation with humans offers the best path forward for both races. They see human energy and innovation as complementary to elven wisdom and longevity, a partnership that could achieve more than either race alone. They also pragmatically recognize that humans now outnumber elves many times over, making conflict an increasingly poor option.

Diplomats staff the outposts where elves interact with the outside world, trading elven goods for human manufactures and establishing relationships with human merchants and nobles. They serve as ambassadors and mediators, working to resolve conflicts before they escalate and building bridges across the cultural divide.

This approach puts the Diplomats at odds with their isolationist kin, who view any cooperation with humans as collaboration with the enemy. Internal elven politics are complex and often bloody, with diplomatic missions sometimes sabotaged by isolationist agents. Those who work closely with the Diplomats may find themselves caught in the crossfire.

title: The Goblin Clans
text:
The Goblin Clans inhabit the wild places that civilized races have not claimed: caves, ruins, and the desolate reaches where life is hard but interference is rare. Viewed as pests by most, goblins are actually organized into complex clan structures with their own politics, traditions, and culture. They simply operate by different rules than their larger neighbors.

Goblin society rewards cunning over strength, with clan leaders rising through schemes and alliances rather than simple combat. This makes goblin politics a treacherous maze where today's ally becomes tomorrow's enemy and betrayal is simply another tool. External observers often mistake this flexibility for chaos, missing the patterns that govern goblin behavior.

Some goblin clans have sought legitimacy through trade, offering services and goods that civilized merchants cannot or will not provide. Others have embraced banditry, raiding caravans and settlements for plunder. Most fall somewhere between these extremes, trading when it benefits them and raiding when it does not. Earning goblin trust requires understanding this pragmatic worldview.

title: The Tenger Horde
text:
The Tenger Horde sweeps out of the southern wastes like a storm of blood and fire, a human subgroup whose warrior culture has made them feared throughout the realm. They have razed villages, slaughtered garrisons, and left nothing but ashes and skulls in their wake. To the Tengers, strength is the only virtue and mercy is weakness.

Tenger society organizes around warlords who rule through personal combat prowess and the loyalty of their warriors. Challenges for leadership are common and always fatal, ensuring that only the strongest survive to command. They despise magic as the tool of cowards and kill any spellcasters they capture after prolonged torture.

Despite their brutality, the Tengers follow a code of honor incomprehensible to outsiders. They respect courage even in enemies, sometimes sparing those who fight well. They keep oaths sworn before their war gods, and breaking such an oath means death. Navigating Tenger culture requires understanding which rules apply in which circumstances, knowledge that few outsiders possess.
